title: DeepLearning.AI Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) Course
description: Comprehensive 24.5-hour course covering RAG overview, information retrieval foundations, vector databases with Weaviate, LLMs and text generation, and RAG systems in production. Taught by Zain Hasan with 49 videos, 9 code examples, and 10 graded assignments. Builds domain-specific chatbots and implements hybrid search.

title: Boot.dev Learn Retrieval Augmented Generation
description: 40-hour, 75-lesson course that implements search techniques from scratch in Python. Covers TF-IDF, BM25 keyword search, semantic search with embeddings, chunking, hybrid search, LLMs, reranking, evaluation, agentic RAG, and multimodal RAG. Uses the Gemini API and teaches the full RAG stack.

title: Coursera Generative AI Applications with RAG and LangChain
description: IBM-sponsored 9-hour intermediate course covering document loading and text-splitting with LangChain, embedding with watsonx, storing in ChromaDB/FAISS, advanced retrievers (Multi-Query, Self-Query, Parent Document), and building a QA bot with Gradio. Includes comparison of RAG vs. fine-tuning with InstructLab.

title: Educative.io Fundamentals of RAG with LangChain
description: 3-hour beginner course covering RAG architecture and workflows, indexing techniques, retrieval strategies, building end-to-end RAG pipelines with LangChain, and creating interactive web apps with Streamlit. Also covers transitioning between vector stores and an advanced challenge building RAG with Google Gemini.

title: LangChain Official RAG Agent Tutorial
description: Official LangChain tutorial demonstrating two RAG approaches: a RAG Agent with tool-based retrieval and a RAG Chain with single LLM call per query. Shows how to use the create_agent API, @tool decorator, and vector store similarity search in about 40 lines of code. Supports multiple LLM providers and vector stores.

title: Qdrant Vector Database
description: High-performance open-source vector database written in Rust, supporting dense vectors, sparse vectors (SPLADE/BM25), and multi-vector late interaction (ColBERT). Offers the best price-to-performance ratio among vector databases, with advanced payload filtering, quantization, and a flexible Query API for hybrid search with RRF and DBSF fusion.

title: Weaviate Vector Database
description: Open-source vector database with native BM25 + vector hybrid search, modular AI stack (vectorizers, generative modules, RAG modules), multi-tenancy support, and GraphQL API. Excels at enterprise RAG workloads requiring keyword and semantic search together. Available as self-hosted or managed Weaviate Cloud.

title: ChromaDB Embedded Vector Database
description: Lightweight Python-native vector database with an embedded mode requiring no server. Provides dead-simple API, zero configuration, and excellent LangChain/LlamaIndex integration. Best for RAG prototyping, notebooks, and small internal tools. Not designed for production scale or multi-user concurrent access.

title: Vector Database Selection Decision Framework
description: Decision framework for choosing the right vector database based on scale, operational requirements, and use case. Prototyping: ChromaDB. Production under 1M vectors without DevOps: Pinecone. Cost-sensitive production with 10-100M vectors: Qdrant. Enterprise hybrid search: Weaviate. Billions of vectors with K8s: Milvus. Existing PostgreSQL: pgvector. Migration path from dev to production scale.

title: Build a Basic RAG Pipeline End-to-End
description: Step-by-step workflow for building a minimal RAG pipeline from document ingestion to query answering. Covers the five core stages of RAG: loading documents, chunking text, generating embeddings, storing in a vector database, and retrieving plus generating answers. Uses LangChain and ChromaDB for the simplest possible production-quality pipeline.
workflow: Install dependencies: pip install langchain langchain-chroma langchain-openai
Load documents using document loaders (PDF, web, text, etc.)
Split documents into chunks using RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter (chunk_size=1000, chunk_overlap=200)
Generate embeddings using OpenAI text-embedding-3-small or HuggingFace all-MiniLM-L6-v2
Store chunks and embeddings in ChromaDB vector store using from_documents()
Create a retriever from the vector store with as_retriever(search_type='similarity', k=5)
Build a RAG chain combining the retriever with an LLM prompt that includes retrieved context
Query the chain with user questions and verify answers cite the retrieved context

title: Set Up Qdrant Hybrid Search with Dense and Sparse Vectors
description: Complete workflow for implementing hybrid search in Qdrant combining dense vector semantic search with sparse vector keyword matching (BM25/SPLADE), fused using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). This pattern significantly outperforms either dense or sparse search alone, especially for queries containing specific terms or IDs.
workflow: Install: pip install qdrant-client[fastembed] fastembed
Create a Qdrant collection with both dense and sparse vector configurations
Configure dense vectors (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2, 384 dimensions, cosine distance)
Configure sparse vectors with SparseVectorParams for BM25 or SPLADE
Index documents using client.upsert() with both dense and sparse vector representations
For search, use client.query_points() with two Prefetch queries (one dense, one sparse)
Apply FusionQuery(fusion=models.Fusion.RRF) to merge results from both prefetched queries
Optionally add ColBERT late-interaction reranking as a third stage for maximum precision
Monitor recall@k and latency metrics; tune the k limits on each prefetch stage

title: Evaluate RAG Quality with RAGAS Metrics
description: Workflow for systematically evaluating RAG pipeline quality using the RAGAS framework. Covers creating evaluation datasets, computing faithfulness, answer relevancy, context precision, and context recall metrics, and interpreting results. Emphasizes scoring retrieval and generation separately to catch silent regressions.
workflow: Install RAGAS: pip install ragas and ragas quickstart rag_eval
Prepare an evaluation dataset with question, ground_truth answer, and contexts columns
Run your RAG pipeline on the evaluation questions to collect generated answers and retrieved contexts
Define metrics: Faithfulness(), FactualCorrectness(), ResponseRelevancy(), LLMContextRecall()
Wrap your LLM with LangchainLLMWrapper or use llm_factory() for the judge model
Call evaluate(dataset, metrics=metrics, llm=evaluator_llm) to compute all metric scores
Interpret scores: 0.8-1.0 excellent, 0.6-0.8 good, 0.4-0.6 moderate, below 0.4 needs improvement
Score retrieval and generation separately: context metrics for retrieval, faithfulness for generation
Set up CI/CD gates: block deployments if faithfulness drops below 0.85 or context precision below 0.7

title: Build a GraphRAG Pipeline with Microsoft GraphRAG
description: Workflow for building a knowledge-graph-enhanced RAG system using Microsoft GraphRAG. Covers installation, configuration, indexing pipeline (chunking, entity extraction, relationship extraction, community detection, summarization), and querying with global, local, and DRIFT search methods. Enables answering holistic questions that standard vector RAG cannot.
workflow: Install: pip install graphrag
Initialize: graphrag init (creates settings.yaml, .env, input/ directory)
Configure API key in .env: GRAPHRAG_API_KEY=sk-...
Place source documents (.txt, .csv, .json) in the input/ directory
Run prompt tuning: graphrag prompt-tune (auto-generates domain-optimized extraction prompts)
Run the indexing pipeline: graphrag index (chunks docs, extracts entities/relationships, builds knowledge graph, detects communities, generates summaries)
Query with global search for holistic questions: graphrag query --method global 'What are the main themes?'
Query with local search for entity-specific questions: graphrag query --method local 'Who is Scrooge and what are his relationships?'
Query with DRIFT search for multi-level reasoning: graphrag query --method drift 'How does Scrooge's character develop?'
Monitor costs: use gpt-4o-mini for testing, enable caching, use --method fast for cheaper indexing

title: Deploy Production RAG Monitoring with OpenTelemetry and Langfuse
description: Workflow for setting up comprehensive observability for a production RAG system using OpenTelemetry tracing and Langfuse for cost/latency/quality monitoring. Covers per-stage latency breakdown, token cost tracking, implicit quality signals, and alerting thresholds. Ensures silent regressions in answer quality are caught before they impact users.
workflow: Install Langfuse SDK: pip install langfuse
Instrument your RAG pipeline with @observe() decorators on each stage (embed, retrieve, rerank, generate)
Log structured JSON request data: per-stage latency, token counts, model names, cache hits, chunk IDs
Track cost per request using token_counts * model_pricing_table
Set up a Postgres-backed rag_requests table for querying daily cost, p99 latency, cache hit rates
Define SLO thresholds: retrieval p95 < 200ms, LLM p95 < 3s, faithfulness > 0.85
Configure alerting on implicit quality signals: 'I don't know' rate, answer length distribution, empty chunk IDs
Run periodic RAGAS evaluation on a fixed test set to catch quality regressions
Version your embedding model and store the version alongside each vector; plan for shadow re-indexing on model changes

title: LangChain RAG Agent with Tool-Based Retrieval
description: Minimal working RAG agent using LangChain's create_agent API with a retrieval tool decorator. Demonstrates the recommended 2025 pattern of building RAG as an agent with tool-based retrieval rather than a single-chain call, enabling the LLM to decide when and how to retrieve context. Uses InMemoryVectorStore and OpenAI embeddings.
code:
import bs4
from langchain.agents import create_agent
from langchain.tools import tool
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI, OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_core.vectorstores import InMemoryVectorStore

# 1. Load and chunk documents
text_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=1000, chunk_overlap=200)
all_splits = text_splitter.split_documents(docs)

# 2. Index into vector store
vector_store = InMemoryVectorStore.from_documents(
    documents=all_splits,
    embedding=OpenAIEmbeddings()
)

# 3. Define retrieval tool
@tool(response_format="content_and_artifact")
def retrieve_context(query: str):
    """Retrieve information from the knowledge base."""
    retrieved_docs = vector_store.similarity_search(query, k=4)
    serialized = "\n\n".join(
        f"Source: {doc.metadata}\nContent: {doc.page_content}"
        for doc in retrieved_docs
    )
    return serialized, retrieved_docs

# 4. Create RAG agent
model = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
agent = create_agent(model, [retrieve_context])

# 5. Query
for step in agent.stream({"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is task decomposition?"}]}):
    step["messages"][-1].pretty_print()

title: Qdrant Hybrid Search with RRF Fusion
description: Complete working example of hybrid search in Qdrant combining dense vectors (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) with sparse vectors (BM25) using Reciprocal Rank Fusion. Uses Qdrant's Query API with FastEmbed for automatic embedding computation. This pattern yields significantly better recall than either dense or sparse search alone.

title: RAGAS RAG Evaluation Pipeline
description: Working code for evaluating a RAG system with RAGAS v0.3+ metrics. Demonstrates the modern evaluate() API with Faithfulness, FactualCorrectness, ResponseRelevancy, and LLMContextRecall. Shows how to create an EvaluationDataset, run evaluation with rate-limit-friendly settings, and interpret scores across quality thresholds.
code:
from ragas import evaluate, EvaluationDataset, RunConfig
from ragas.metrics import (
    Faithfulness,
    FactualCorrectness,
    ResponseRelevancy,
    LLMContextRecall,
    NoiseSensitivity,
)
from ragas.llms import LangchainLLMWrapper
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

# 1. Configure evaluator LLM
evaluator_llm = LangchainLLMWrapper(ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o"))

# 2. Create evaluation dataset
eval_data = [
    {
        "user_input": "What is RAG?",
        "response": "RAG combines retrieval with generation...",
        "reference": "Retrieval-Augmented Generation retrieves relevant documents...",
        "retrieved_contexts": ["RAG retrieves documents from a knowledge base..."]
    }
]
dataset = EvaluationDataset.from_list(eval_data)

# 3. Define metrics
metrics = [
    Faithfulness(),
    FactualCorrectness(),
    ResponseRelevancy(),
    LLMContextRecall(),
    NoiseSensitivity(),
]

# 4. Run evaluation with rate-limit-friendly settings
rate_config = RunConfig(
    timeout=300,
    max_retries=15,
    max_wait=90,
    max_workers=4,
    log_tenacity=True
)

results = evaluate(
    dataset=dataset,
    metrics=metrics,
    llm=evaluator_llm,
    run_config=rate_config
)

# 5. Interpret results
# 0.8-1.0: Excellent, 0.6-0.8: Good, 0.4-0.6: Moderate, <0.4: Needs improvement
print(results)

title: SPLADE Sparse Retrieval with Sentence Transformers
description: Working code for SPLADE sparse neural retrieval using the Sentence Transformers SparseEncoder API. Demonstrates encoding a corpus with SPLADE, performing semantic search, and inspecting which tokens influenced each result. SPLADE produces interpretable sparse representations where each dimension corresponds to a vocabulary term with an importance weight.
code:
from sentence_transformers import SparseEncoder
from sentence_transformers.util import semantic_search

# 1. Load SPLADE model
model = SparseEncoder("naver/splade-v3-distilbert")

# 2. Encode corpus
corpus = [
    "Retrieval-Augmented Generation combines information retrieval with text generation.",
    "Vector databases store high-dimensional embeddings for similarity search.",
    "Hybrid search combines keyword and semantic matching for better recall.",
    "Chunking strategies determine how documents are split for embedding.",
    "Reranking improves retrieval precision by scoring candidates with a cross-encoder.",
]
corpus_embeddings = model.encode(corpus, convert_to_tensor=True)

# 3. Query and search
queries = ["How does RAG work?"]
query_embeddings = model.encode(queries, convert_to_tensor=True)

results = semantic_search(
    query_embeddings,
    corpus_embeddings,
    top_k=3,
    score_function=model.similarity
)

# 4. Inspect influential tokens per result
for query_id, query in enumerate(queries):
    pointwise_scores = model.intersection(
        query_embeddings[query_id], corpus_embeddings
    )
    for res in results[query_id]:
        corpus_id = res["corpus_id"]
        score = res["score"]
        token_scores = model.decode(pointwise_scores[corpus_id], top_k=10)
        print(f"Score: {score:.4f} - {corpus[corpus_id][:60]}...")
        print(f"  Top tokens: {token_scores}")

title: LlamaIndex Semantic Chunking Pipeline
description: Working code for semantic chunking with LlamaIndex using the SemanticSplitterNodeParser. Splits documents adaptively based on embedding similarity between sentences rather than fixed chunk sizes, ensuring each chunk contains semantically related content. Includes parameter tuning for buffer_size and breakpoint_percentile_threshold.
code:
from llama_index.core import SimpleDirectoryReader
from llama_index.core.node_parser import SemanticSplitterNodeParser
from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding

# 1. Load documents
documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./data").load_data()

# 2. Configure semantic splitter
embed_model = OpenAIEmbedding(model="text-embedding-3-small")
semantic_splitter = SemanticSplitterNodeParser(
    buffer_size=1,                        # Sentences per buffer (1 = most sensitive)
    breakpoint_percentile_threshold=95,   # Higher = fewer, larger chunks
    embed_model=embed_model
)

# 3. Split into semantically coherent nodes
nodes = semantic_splitter.get_nodes_from_documents(documents)

# 4. Inspect chunks
for i, node in enumerate(nodes[:5]):
    print(f"Chunk {i} ({len(node.text)} chars):")
    print(f"  {node.text[:200]}...")
    print()

# 5. Build vector index from semantic nodes
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex
index = VectorStoreIndex(nodes, embed_model=embed_model)

# 6. Query
query_engine = index.as_query_engine()
response = query_engine.query("What are the main themes?")
print(response)

title: Advanced Retrieval: Hybrid Search with RRF and DBSF Fusion
description: Pattern for combining dense vector search with sparse keyword matching using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) or Distribution-Based Score Fusion (DBSF) in Qdrant. RRF is robust and parameter-free; DBSF provides score normalization when dense and sparse scores have different distributions. Start with RRF, add ColBERT reranking for precision-critical use cases.

title: Cascading Retrieval-Reranking Pipeline (SPLADE to Cross-Encoder to LLM)
description: Production retrieval pattern using a three-stage cascading pipeline: SPLADE or BM25 for first-stage candidate generation, cross-encoder (DeBERTa-v3) for efficient reranking of top-k candidates, and GPT-4 for listwise reranking of the final top-10. Achieves nDCG@10 of 63.7 on DL23, outperforming any single reranker. Cross-encoders remain competitive with LLM-based rerankers at far lower cost.

title: ColBERT Late Interaction Reranking Pattern
description: Pattern for using ColBERT's MaxSim late interaction scoring as a reranking stage after initial candidate retrieval. ColBERT produces multi-vector token-level embeddings and computes fine-grained similarity via maximum similarity operations. Best used as a reranker on top 100-500 candidates from BM25 or dense retrieval, not as a first-stage retriever. Supports memory-mapped indexes for 90% RAM reduction.

title: Recursive Semantic Chunking (RSC) Pattern
description: Advanced chunking pattern that recursively splits large chunks and merges small ones to maintain semantic coherence. Starts with initial semantic chunking in embedding space, then recursively splits chunks exceeding a threshold (T_chunk=1500 chars) with decreasing delta, merges short chunks (below T_merge=350 chars) with their most similar neighbor, and applies final size adjustment. Outperforms fixed and basic semantic chunking on contextual relevancy metrics.

title: Pseudo-Instruction Chunking (PIC) Pattern
description: Chunking pattern that uses document summaries as pseudo-instructions to guide chunk boundaries. Without knowing real user queries in advance, summaries serve as proxies for the types of questions users might ask. Groups sentences whose embeddings are similar to the summary embedding into coherent chunks. Achieves highest average retrieval performance across 6 benchmarks. Validates that aligning chunks with query intent significantly improves retrieval.

title: Multi-Granularity Chunking with Parent-Child Retrieval
description: Chunking and retrieval pattern that creates parent chunks (large, e.g. 2000 tokens) and child chunks (small, e.g. 500 tokens) at multiple granularities. Retrieves using small child chunks for precision but returns the parent chunk for full context. Score of a parent chunk equals the max score of its child chunks. LGMGC implements this with LLM logits-guided boundaries, achieving best performance and smallest variance across chunk size hyperparameters.

title: Fixed-Size vs Recursive vs Semantic Chunking Decision Guide
description: Practical decision guide for choosing chunking strategies based on use case. Fixed-size: lowest recall, best for quick prototyping. Recursive character (400-512 tokens, 10-20% overlap): 85-90% recall, best default starting point. Sentence-based: strong with ColBERT, best for Q&A and transcripts. Page-level: sleeper pick for PDFs with tables/figures. Semantic: up to 70% improvement, best for high-value content. LLM-based: highest recall (0.913) but highest cost.

title: OpenAI Embeddings (text-embedding-3-small/large)
description: OpenAI's embedding models: text-embedding-3-small ($0.02/1M tokens, 1536 dims) is the cheapest managed option and best default for new projects. text-embedding-3-large ($0.13/1M tokens, 3072 dims) offers higher quality at 6.5x cost. Both support Matryoshka dimension reduction (truncate to 256-1536 dims without re-embedding). No update in over 2 years; weaker multilingual performance vs. dedicated models; 8191-token context limit.

title: BGE-M3 Multilingual Embedding Model
description: State-of-the-art open-source multilingual embedding model supporting 100+ languages with three retrieval methods in one model: dense, sparse (BM25-like), and multi-vector (ColBERT). Achieves best multilingual performance on MIRACL and MKQA benchmarks. 1024 dimensions, 8192-token context. Requires GPU for inference; self-hosting breaks even with commercial APIs at 9-18M tokens/month. No dimension flexibility.

title: Embedding Model Selection Decision Framework
description: Decision framework for choosing embedding models. Start with OpenAI text-embedding-3-small for new English projects (cheapest, zero infra). Choose BGE-M3 for multilingual/self-hosted. Choose Cohere embed-v4 for enterprise SLAs and 128K context. Choose E5-Mistral-7B for maximum open-source quality with long documents. Choose Voyage AI for domain-specialized retrieval. Critical: switching models requires full re-embedding of entire corpus.

title: RAGAS Evaluation Framework
description: The most popular RAG evaluation framework (13.3K GitHub stars) for automated quality assessment. v0.3+ introduces DiscreteMetric for pass/fail scoring, @experiment() decorator for evaluation loops, and llm_factory() for easy LLM setup. Core metrics: Faithfulness, FactualCorrectness, ResponseRelevancy, LLMContextRecall, ContextPrecision, NoiseSensitivity. Uses dual-judge averaging for more granular scoring. Supports OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Ollama.

title: DeepEval RAG Evaluation and CI/CD
description: Comprehensive LLM evaluation framework (14.7K GitHub stars) with 50+ metrics covering RAG, agents, MCP, and safety. Native Pytest integration for CI/CD gates. Supports both reference-free and reference-based evaluation. Notable: tends to under-score golden contexts (mean 0.46 vs. 0.82-0.91 for other tools) due to statement decomposition penalizing off-topic sentences. Best for teams wanting evaluation-as-code with CI/CD enforcement.

title: Separate Retrieval and Generation Scoring
description: Critical production pattern: score retrieval and generation quality separately, never mix them into a single 'answer quality' metric. Retrieval metrics (context precision, context recall, context relevance) measure whether the right information was found. Generation metrics (faithfulness, answer relevance) measure whether the LLM used that information correctly. A RAG system can score 0.95 faithfulness and still produce wrong answers if retrieved content is stale or incorrect.

title: Langfuse Open-Source RAG Observability
description: Open-source (MIT license) observability platform for RAG systems built on OpenTelemetry, avoiding vendor lock-in. Offers free self-hosting via Docker Compose with EU data region options. Data model: Sessions, Traces, Observations with nesting. Component-level evaluation for testing different chunking strategies independently. Experiment Runner for running datasets against configurations. Lowest per-unit cost at scale ($6/100k units at 50M+). Background processing for non-blocking trace transmission.

title: Production RAG Cost Optimization Playbook
description: Systematic cost optimization playbook achieving 80-90% cost reduction for production RAG systems. Key strategies: deduplication (40-70% embedding savings), batch processing (50% API cost savings), float8 quantization (75% storage reduction with less than 0.3% accuracy loss), caching (60-80% hit rates reducing latency from 150ms to less than 20ms), context-aware chunking (80-85% token usage reduction), and conditional reranking (skip for high-confidence queries, 40-60% fewer invocations). Total cost of ownership for 10M documents drops from $482/month to $187/month.

title: Production RAG Monitoring: Per-Stage Latency Breakdown and SLOs
description: Pattern for monitoring production RAG systems with per-stage latency breakdowns and SLO definitions. Target p95 retrieval less than 200ms, p95 LLM generation less than 3s. LLM inference dominates the latency budget (~2.5s of ~2.7s total). Use OpenTelemetry plus Prometheus plus Grafana for end-to-end tracing. Key signals: per-stage latency, token counts, cache hit rates, 'I don't know' rate, answer length distribution. Use head-based sampling (1/1000) for high-volume embedding calls and tail-based sampling for expensive LLM inference.

title: RAG Data Versioning and Regression Testing
description: Production pattern for versioning document stores and embedding models alongside RAG pipelines. Uses lakeFS for data versioning, stores embedding commit hashes alongside vectors, logs model names/versions with every request, and periodically re-runs fixed evaluation sets to catch regressions. Key principle: treat your vector store like application code, with version control, rollback capability, and CI/CD testing. Ensures that model upgrades, re-chunking, or re-embedding don't silently degrade retrieval quality.

title: Microsoft GraphRAG
description: Microsoft's open-source GraphRAG library (32.8K+ GitHub stars) that chunks documents, extracts entities and relationships, builds knowledge graphs, detects communities, and generates summaries. Supports three query methods: global (holistic, dataset-wide questions via map-reduce over community summaries), local (entity-specific questions), and DRIFT (multi-level reasoning combining depth and breadth). Uses GPT-4o-mini by default with prompt tuning for domain optimization.

title: Neo4j GraphRAG Python Library
description: Official Neo4j GraphRAG Python package (1.1K+ GitHub stars) providing SimpleKGPipeline for knowledge graph construction, multiple retrievers (VectorRetriever, VectorCypherRetriever, Text2CypherRetriever, Hybrid retrievers), and a GraphRAG class for end-to-end Q&A. Supports multiple LLM backends (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, Google, Cohere, Mistral, Bedrock) and vector DB integrations (Weaviate, Pinecone, Qdrant). Extensible TextSplitter base class for custom chunking.

title: GraphRAG vs Vector RAG Decision Framework
description: Decision pattern for choosing between vector RAG and GraphRAG. Use vector RAG when: queries are specific and local, you need fast simple answers, and corpus is well-structured. Use GraphRAG when: queries require holistic understanding across documents, you need to trace relationships between entities, you need explainable reasoning paths, and your data is rich in entities and relationships. GraphRAG indexing costs 20-40x more than vector indexing but enables answering global questions that vector RAG cannot.

title: Microsoft GraphRAG Python API: Index, Query, and Search
description: Working code for Microsoft GraphRAG's Python API showing how to programmatically build indexes, run global/local/DRIFT searches, and generate domain-specific prompts. Covers the full workflow from configuration to entity extraction, community detection, and multi-level querying. Uses the async API for production deployments.
code:
from graphrag.api import build_index, global_search, local_search, drift_search
from graphrag.config.models.graph_rag_config import GraphRagConfig

# 1. Load configuration
config = GraphRagConfig.from_file("./my-project/settings.yaml")

# 2. Build index (chunks docs, extracts entities/relationships,
#    builds knowledge graph, detects communities, generates summaries)
results = await build_index(config=config, verbose=True)

# 3. Global Search - holistic, dataset-wide questions
response, context = await global_search(
    config=config,
    entities=results.entities,
    communities=results.communities,
    community_reports=results.community_reports,
    community_level=2,
    query="What are the main themes across all documents?",
    response_type="Multiple Paragraphs"
)

# 4. Local Search - entity-focused questions
response, context = await local_search(
    config=config,
    entities=results.entities,
    text_units=results.text_units,
    relationships=results.relationships,
    query="Tell me about the relationship between X and Y"
)

# 5. DRIFT Search - multi-level reasoning
response, context = await drift_search(
    config=config,
    entities=results.entities,
    query="How do different themes relate across the corpus?"
)

print(response)

title: RAG in Production with Haystack
description: O'Reilly published practical blueprint (132 pages, April 2025) for building RAG applications using the open-source Haystack framework. Covers extractive and generative QA, semantic search, and building secure ChatGPT-like experiences on technical documentation. Provides LLM software development lifecycle mental models for production RAG systems.

title: Mastering Retrieval-Augmented Generation
description: Comprehensive 847-page Apress guide covering enterprise-grade RAG systems from architectural patterns to production deployment. Addresses scalable deployment with security, privacy, and governance; evaluation frameworks and quality assurance; advanced retrieval strategies including graph-based and agentic systems; and real-world applications in regulatory compliance, customer support, and code generation. Published January 2026.

title: Hands-On RAG for Production
description: Comprehensive O'Reilly guide (400 pages, June 2026) by Ofer Mendelevitch and Forrest Bao covering the full roadmap for building, refining, and scaling production-grade RAG applications. Topics include data ingestion, embeddings, vector search, advanced techniques (multimodal RAG, agentic RAG, GraphRAG), and enterprise deployment challenges (security, privacy, explainability). Includes build vs. RAG-as-a-service decision frameworks.

title: RAG-Critic: Automated Error Identification and Correction in RAG
description: ACL 2025 paper proposing a hierarchical RAG error taxonomy (3 tiers, 4000+ error types) and a critic-guided agentic workflow. The Generate-Critic-Plan-Execute loop identifies errors, selects from 5 action functions (Retrieval, Rewrite, Decompose, Refine, Generate), and corrects them. A 3B critic model outperforms 70B models on fine-grained error identification (58.3% vs 31.5%). Achieves +2.8% to +5.3% F1 improvement over Self-RAG, FLARE, and MetaRAG baselines.

title: R3-RAG: Reinforcement Learning for Step-by-Step Retrieval
description: Agentic RAG pattern using RL to train LLMs to iteratively reason and retrieve step-by-step. Combines outcome rewards (answer correctness) and process rewards (relevance-based document verification at each retrieval step). Uses PPO training with cold start from SFT on synthesized interleaved reasoning-retrieval trajectories. Outperforms IRCoT by approximately 15 percentage points on multi-hop QA. Uses 22.8% fewer tokens than ReSearch while achieving higher accuracy. Strong transferability across retrievers (BM25, E5, BGE) with less than 3% variation.

title: CoRAG: Chain-of-Retrieval Augmented Generation
description: Agentic RAG pattern that trains o1-like RAG models to dynamically reformulate queries step-by-step through rejection sampling-generated intermediate retrieval chains. Supports test-time compute scaling via greedy decoding, best-of-N sampling, and tree search strategies. The model learns to decompose complex queries and reformulate queries upon retrieval failure. Achieves greater than 10 points EM improvement over strong baselines on multi-hop QA and new SOTA on the KILT benchmark. Demonstrates log-linear scaling between token consumption and performance.

title: SIM-RAG: Self-Practicing When to Stop Retrieving
description: Agentic RAG pattern addressing the critical challenge of when to stop retrieving in multi-round RAG. Trains a lightweight Critic (783M or 2.85B Flan-T5) to assess information sufficiency using self-practiced synthetic data. Even the lightweight SIM-RAGlite outperforms Self-RAG on HotpotQA (27.8% vs 17.1% EM) with 1/10th the parameters. Key finding: a specialized lightweight Critic outperforms GPT-4-as-Critic for complex multi-hop tasks. Avoids both over-confidence and over-retrieval.

title: UniversalRAG: Modality-Aware Routing for Multimodal Retrieval
description: Multimodal RAG pattern that dynamically identifies the most appropriate modality-specific corpus (text, image, table, video) and performs targeted retrieval. Key finding: forcing all modalities into a unified embedding space causes a modality gap where retrieval favors items of the same modality as the query. Routing to modality-specific corpora avoids this gap. Supports both training-based routers (Qwen3-VL-2B, InternVL3.5-1B) and training-free routers (GPT-5). Multi-granularity retrieval (paragraphs vs. documents, clips vs. full videos) consistently improves performance.

title: mRAG Design Space for Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation
description: First systematic dissection of the multimodal RAG pipeline for large vision-language models. Investigates retrieval phase configurations (image-only, image+text, image+caption, caption-only), reranking strategies (pointwise, pairwise, listwise), and generation integration. Key findings: EVA-CLIP is the most robust zero-shot retriever; listwise reranking with LVLMs is the best zero-shot strategy (+2.6% Recall@1); adding more documents does NOT improve generation accuracy; an agentic framework with self-reflection boosts response accuracy by +5%/+2% on E-VQA/InfoSeek.

title: Multimodal RAG Survey: Scaling Beyond Context for Document Understanding
description: Comprehensive survey of 45+ multimodal RAG methods for document understanding covering text, tables, charts, and images. Organizes approaches by domain (open vs. closed), retrieval modality (image-only vs. image+text), granularity (page-level vs. element-level), and hybrid enhancements (graph-based, agent-based). Key trends: shift from page-level to element-level retrieval, multimodal knowledge graphs (mKG-RAG), and multi-agent workflows (ViDoRAG, HM-RAG).

title: Udacity: Large Language Models (LLMs) and RAG
description: Udacity course covering LLM fundamentals and RAG architecture with a NASA capstone project. Covers embeddings, vector search, retrieval strategies, generation, and evaluation.

title: Activeloop Gen AI 360: RAG Course (Free)
description: Free RAG course from the Gen AI 360 series covering retrieval fundamentals, vector databases, embedding models, chunking strategies, and end-to-end RAG pipeline construction with Deep Lake.

title: Production RAG Pipeline Architecture: 8-Stage End-to-End Workflow
description: Comprehensive production RAG architecture splitting into offline data pipeline (ingest-chunk-embed-index) and online query pipeline (retrieve-rerank-augment-generate), with semantic caching, evaluation harnesses, and cost benchmarks. Covers the full lifecycle from document ingestion through generation with production-grade observability.
workflow: INGEST & PARSE: Strip boilerplate with Unstructured.io/LlamaParse; capture metadata (source URL, section path, content hash); make ingestion incremental on hash change
CHUNK: Structure-aware recursive splitting (H2 > H3 > paragraph > sentence); 400-800 tokens with 10-20% overlap; prepend document title and section path to each chunk
EMBED: Batch embedding during ingestion (never one-at-a-time); default to text-embedding-3-small ($0.02/1M tokens); self-host BGE-M3 for privacy workloads
INDEX: Store in pgvector (default for <5M vectors) or Qdrant (multi-tenancy); HNSW with M=16-32, ef_construction=64-200; track is_current flag for soft deletes
RETRIEVE: Hybrid dense + BM25 with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF); retrieve k=20-50 candidates; add metadata filters for multi-tenant security; HyDE for recall gains
RERANK: Cross-encoder rescore top 20-50 candidates (Cohere rerank-3.5 or BGE-reranker); 8-14pp recall improvement for 50-200ms latency cost
GENERATE: Prompt budgeting (system > context in XML tags > history > query last); strict grounding instruction; stream tokens for TTFT < 1s; structured output with JSON schemas
CACHE & EVALUATE: Semantic cache at 0.92 similarity threshold (~31% hit rate); evaluation harness with 50-400 curated pairs tracking recall@K, faithfulness, citation accuracy; wire into CI

title: RAG Pipeline Orchestration with Kestra
description: Practical guide to orchestrating RAG pipelines (ingestion and query) using Kestra workflow engine, covering document processing, embedding, vector store indexing, and query-time retrieval with reranking and generation.
workflow: DEFINE INGESTION FLOW: Create Kestra flow with document source trigger (S3, GCS, SFTP, HTTP); parse documents with Unstructured.io connector
CHUNK & EMBED: Apply recursive character splitting with overlap; batch embed with configurable model; validate embedding dimensions match vector store schema
INDEX TO VECTOR STORE: Upsert embeddings with metadata to Qdrant/Pinecone/Weaviate; handle deduplication via content hash; set up scheduled reconciliation for deletions
DEFINE QUERY FLOW: Accept user query via API trigger; embed query; execute hybrid search (dense + keyword) with configurable top-k; fuse results via RRF
RERANK & GENERATE: Rerank candidates with cross-encoder; assemble prompt with retrieved context; call LLM with streaming; return answer with citations
MONITOR & EVALUATE: Log all flow executions with latency per stage; set up evaluation dataset comparison; alert on quality regression

title: Langfuse Observation-Level Evaluation for RAG Pipelines
description: Langfuse v4 (2026) introduces observation-level evaluations that run on individual operations (retrieval, generation, tool calls) rather than entire traces. Enables compositional evaluation (toxicity on outputs, relevance on retrievals), asynchronous processing at ingest time, and order-of-magnitude faster queries via a single immutable Observations table.

title: Production Semantic Caching for LLM Applications: TTLs, Confidence, and Cache Safety
description: Production-hardening pattern for semantic caches using FastAPI + Redis + Embeddings. Covers TTL validation at application level, confidence scoring (0.7 similarity + 0.3 freshness), deduplication via query normalization and SHA-256 hashing, and cache poisoning prevention (never cache errors, reject malformed entries). Prioritizes correctness over speed.
workflow: QUERY NORMALIZATION: Lowercase, collapse whitespace, compute SHA-256 hash; check exact-match cache first (fastest path, ~42ms response time)
SEMANTIC SIMILARITY: Embed normalized query; search Redis vector store with cosine similarity; retrieve top-k cached entries above minimum threshold
CONFIDENCE SCORING: Compute confidence = 0.7 * similarity + 0.3 * freshness; reject entries below domain-specific confidence floor; fall back to LLM when in doubt
TTL VALIDATION: Apply application-level TTL enforcement (not Redis-level); domain-specific TTLs (factual: 7d, time-sensitive: 1h, creative: never cache)
POISONING PREVENTION: Never cache error responses (e.g., [LLM Error]); reject malformed entries at read time; validate response structure before storage
CACHE UPDATE: Store LLM response with metadata (timestamp, query hash, confidence score); trigger background deduplication to merge paraphrased variants

title: SAFE-CACHE: Adversarial-Resilient Semantic Caching for RAG Systems
description: Research demonstrating that standard semantic caches (GPTCache, Azure Semantic Cache) are vulnerable to embedding-collision attacks (52.77% success rate). SAFE-CACHE replaces single-query matching with cluster-centroid-based caching using Louvain community detection, bi-encoder refinement with adversarial triplet loss, and Q&A combined embeddings to reduce adversarial success to 14.27%.

title: Continuous Semantic Caching for Low-Cost LLM Serving (Microsoft Research)
description: First rigorous theoretical framework for semantic LLM caching in continuous query spaces. Introduces dynamic epsilon-net discretization with Kernel Ridge Regression to quantify uncertainty and generalize partial feedback across semantic neighborhoods. Proves sublinear regret bounds against an optimal continuous oracle for online adaptive algorithms.
